figures.caption: Figure 2: Overview of the document OCR inverse rendering process via different decoding methods.. The model maps a 2D document image to a 1D token sequence for decoding through autoregressive and diffusion-based methods.
Table 2: Breakdown of document parsing on OmniDocBench v1.5 by page type, reported in text edit distance (↓).
Figure 10: A very short table where over-provisioned Full-Attn repeats empty rows.
Figure 12: A long table where short Full-Attn truncates and long Full-Attn repeats tail rows.
Figure 11: A medium-length table where matched Full-Attn remains stable but over-provisioned Full-Attn repeats.
Figure 7: Semantic Shuffle benchmark results across distortion levels.
Figure 15: Diffusion decoding examples for layout generation.

Figure 3: Training of MinerU-Diffusion. Left: the target token sequence is randomly masked to form a partially observed input, and the model predicts only the masked positions under visual and prompt conditioning. Right: the structured block-attention mask used during training, where tokens attend bidirectionally within each block and causally to all preceding blocks, enabling parallel diffusion refinement within blocks while preserving coarse autoregressive structure across blocks.
Figure 14: Additional complete recognition examples in the same format.
Figure 8: Training dynamics across the four stages in our training recipe: Stage 0a, Stage 0b, Stage 1, and Stage 2. For each stage, we report both the loss and the gradient norm.
Table 1: Comprehensive evaluation of document parsing on OmniDocBench v1.5. ↑ denotes higher is better, ↓ denotes lower is better.
Figure 6: Comparison of training dynamics across different curriculum strategies. The two-stage framework achieves smoother optimization and higher final accuracy compared to single-stage baselines.

Figure 1: (a) The confidence threshold controls decoding parallelism in MinerU-Diffusion. Compared to MinerU2.5, our method achieves up to 3.26× speedup. (b) MinerU-Diffusion maintains a strong accuracy–efficiency trade-off, achieving 2.12× speedup with 99.9% and 3.01× speedup with 98.8% relative accuracy. (c) Diffusion decoding progressively reconstructs structured text from masked tokens under visual conditioning: black tokens are confirmed, red tokens are being updated, and yellow tokens remain masked, enabling parallel generation with global consistency, in contrast to autoregressive left-to-right decoding.
Figure 18: Diffusion decoding examples for formula recognition.
Figure 13: Complete recognition examples. Left: original page image. Middle: predicted layout. Right: rendered parsed output.
Figure 5: Visualizing the accuracy-throughput trade-off of different models across different OCR tasks under the w/ GT Layout setting.
Table 4: Comparison of different inference strategies and model types in terms of efficiency (TPF, TPS) and document parsing performance.
Figure 17: Diffusion decoding examples for table recognition.
Figure 4: Threshold sensitivity analysis of TPF, TPS, and accuracy. TPF denotes tokens per forward, and TPS refers to throughput measured on an NVIDIA H200 GPU with a batch size of 1.
Table 3: Comprehensive recognition results on CC-OCR [51], OCRBench v2 [9], and UniMER-Test [42]. ↑ denotes higher is better.
Figure 9: Training curves on a table-only subset.
